## TOWARDS A UNIFIED VIEW OF PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TRANSFER LEARNING

## **Junxian He** _[∗]_

Carnegie Mellon University junxianh@cs.cmu.edu

**Chunting Zhou** _[∗]_ Carnegie Mellon University chuntinz@cs.cmu.edu

## **Xuezhe Ma**

University of Southern California xuezhema@isi.edu

**Taylor Berg-Kirkpatrick** UC San Diego tberg@eng.ucsd.edu

**Graham Neubig** Carnegie Mellon University gneubig@cs.cmu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Fine-tuning large pretrained language models on downstream tasks has become the de-facto learning paradigm in NLP. However, conventional approaches finetune all the parameters of the pretrained model, which becomes prohibitive as the model size and the number of tasks grow. Recent work has proposed a variety of parameter-efficient transfer learning methods that only fine-tune a small number of (extra) parameters to attain strong performance. While effective, the critical ingredients for success and the connections among the various methods are poorly understood. In this paper, we break down the design of state-of-the-art parameter-efficient transfer learning methods and present a unified framework that establishes connections between them. Specifically, we re-frame them as modifications to specific hidden states in pretrained models, and define a set of design dimensions along which different methods vary, such as the function to compute the modification and the position to apply the modification. Through comprehensive empirical studies across machine translation, text summarization, language understanding, and text classification benchmarks, we utilize the unified view to identify important design choices in previous methods. Furthermore, our unified framework enables the transfer of design elements across different approaches, and as a result we are able to instantiate new parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that tune less parameters than previous methods while being more effective, achieving comparable results to fine-tuning all parameters on all four tasks.[1]

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Transfer learning from pre-trained language models (PLMs) is now the prevalent paradigm in natural language processing, yielding strong performance on many tasks (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2020). The most common way to adapt general-purpose PLMs to downstream tasks is to fine-tune all the model parameters ( _full fine-tuning_ ). However, this results in a separate copy of fine-tuned model parameters for each task, which is prohibitively expensive when serving models that perform a large number of tasks. This issue is particularly salient with the ever-increasing size of PLMs, which now range from hundreds of millions (Radford et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020) to hundreds of billions (Brown et al., 2020) or even trillions of parameters (Fedus et al., 2021).

To mitigate this issue, a few lightweight alternatives have been proposed to update only a small number of extra parameters while keeping most pretrained parameters frozen. For example, _adapter tuning_ (Houlsby et al., 2019) inserts small neural modules called adapters to each layer of the pretrained network and only the adapters are trained at fine-tuning time. Inspired by the success of prompting methods that control PLMs through textual prompts (Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021a), _prefix tuning_ (Li & Liang, 2021) and _prompt tuning_ (Lester et al., 2021) prepend an additional _l_ tunable

(2021) learn low-rank matrices to approximate parameter updates. We illustrate these methods in Figure 1. These approaches have all been reported to demonstrate comparable performance to full fine-tuning on different sets of tasks, often through updating less than 1% of the original model parameters. Besides parameter savings, parameter-efficient tuning makes it possible to quickly adapt to new tasks without catastrophic forgetting (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) and often exhibits superior robustness in out-of-distribution evaluation (Li & Liang, 2021).

However, we contend that the important ingredients that contribute to the success of these parameterefficient tuning methods are poorly understood, and the connections between them are still unclear. In this paper, we aim to answer three questions: (1) How are these methods connected? (2) Do these methods share design elements that are essential for their effectiveness, and what are they? (3) Can the effective ingredients of each method be transferred to others to yield more effective variants?

In order to answer these questions, we first derive an alternative form of prefix tuning that reveals prefix tuning’s close connections with adapters (§3.1). Based on this we then devise a unified framework that frames the aforementioned methods as different ways to modify the hidden representations of frozen PLMs (§3.2). Our unified framework decomposes previous methods along a _shared_ set of design dimensions, such as the function used to perform the modification, the position in which to impose this modification, and how to integrate the modification. This framework allows us to transfer design choices across approaches to propose new variants such as adapters with multiple heads (§3.3). In experiments, we first show that existing parameter-efficient tuning methods still lag behind full fine-tuning on higher-resource and challenging tasks (§4.2), as exemplified in Figure 2. Then we utilize the unified framework to identify critical design choices and validate the proposed variants empirically (§4.3-4.6). Our experiments on four NLP benchmarks covering text summarization, machine translation (MT), text classification, and general language understanding, demonstrate that the proposed variant uses less parameters than existing methods while being more effective, matching full fine-tuning results on all four tasks.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

## 2.1 RECAP OF THE TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE

The transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017) is now the workhorse architecture behind most stateof-the-art PLMs. In this section we recap the equations of this model for completeness. Transformer models are composed of _L_ stacked blocks, where each block (Figure 1) contains two types of sub-

## 2.2 OVERVIEW OF PREVIOUS PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TUNING METHODS

Below and in Figure 1, we introduce several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Unless otherwise specified, they only tune the added parameters while the PLM’s are frozen.

**Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019):** The adapter approach inserts small modules (adapters) between transformer layers. The adapter layer generally uses a down-projection with _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r]_ to project the input _**h**_ to a lower-dimensional space specified by bottleneck dimension _r_ , followed by a nonlinear activation function _f_ ( _·_ ), and a up-projection with _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][d]_ . These adapters are surrounded by a residual connection, leading to a final form:

_**h** ←_ _**h**_ + _f_ ( _**hW**_ down) _**W**_ up _._ (4)

Houlsby et al. (2019) places two adapters sequentially within one layer of the transformer, one after the multi-head attention and one after the FFN sub-layer. Pfeiffer et al. (2021) have proposed a more efficient adapter variant that is inserted only after the FFN “add & layer norm” sub-layer.

**Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021):** Inspired by the success of textual prompting methods (Liu et al., 2021a), prefix tuning prepends _l_ tunable prefix vectors to the keys and values of the multihead attention at every layer. Specifically, two sets of prefix vectors _**P** k,_ _**P** v ∈_ R _[l][×][d]_ are concatenated with the original key _**K**_ and value _**V**_ . Then multi-head attention is performed on the new prefixed keys and values. The computation of head _i_ in Eq. 2 becomes:

_**P** k_ and _**P** v_ are split into _Nh_ head vectors respectively and _**P** k_[(] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ P]**_[ (] _v[i]_[)] _∈_ R _[l][×][d/N][h]_ denote the _i_ -th head vector. Prompt-tuning (Lester et al., 2021) simplifies prefix-tuning by only prepending to the input word embeddings in the first layer; similar work also includes P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021b).

**LoRA (Hu et al., 2021):** LoRA injects trainable low-rank matrices into transformer layers to approximate the weight updates. For a pre-trained weight matrix _**W** ∈_ R _[d][×][k]_ , LoRA represents its update with a low-rank decomposition _**W**_ +∆ _W_ = _**W**_ + _**W**_ down _**W**_ up, where _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r] ,_ _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][k]_ are tunable parameters. LoRA applies this update to the query and value projection matrices ( _**W** q,_ _**W** v_ ) in the multi-head attention sub-layer, as shown in Figure 1. Further, we note that _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][h][×][l]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[l][×][d][h]_ are lowrank matrices when _l_ is small, and thus they function similarly to the _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up matrices in adapters. This view also suggests that the number of prefix vectors, _l_ , plays a similar role to the bottleneck dimension _r_ in adapters: they both represent the rank limitation of computing the modification vector ∆ _**h**_ . Thus we also refer _l_ as the bottleneck dimension. Intuitively, the rank limitation implies that ∆ _**h**_ is a linear combination of _the same l_ (or _≤ l_ ) basis vectors for any _**x**_ .

**The Difference from Adapters:** In addition to the gating variable _λ_ , we emphasize three differences between prefix tuning and adapters. (1) As demonstrated in Figure 3, prefix tuning uses _**x**_ , the input of the PLM layer, to compute ∆ _**h**_ , while adapters use _**h**_ , the output of the PLM layer. Thus, prefix tuning can be thought of as a “parallel” computation to the PLM layer, whereas the typical adapter is “sequential” computation. (2) Adapters are more flexible with respect to where they are inserted than prefix tuning: adapters typically modify attention or FFN outputs, while prefix tuning only modifies the attention output of each head. Empirically, this makes a large difference as we will show in §4.4. (3) Eq. 10 applies to each attention head, while adapters are always single-headed, which makes prefix tuning more expressive: head attention is of dimension _d/Nh_ – basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if _l ≥ d/Nh_ , but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if _r ≥ d_ . Notably, prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when _l_ = _r_ .[7] We empirically validate such multi-head influence in §4.4.

## 3.2 THE UNIFIED FRAMEWORK

Inspired by the connections between prefix tuning and adapters, we propose a general framework that aims to unify several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Specifically, we cast them as learning a modification vector ∆ _**h**_ , which is applied to various hidden representations. Formally, we denote the hidden representation to be directly modified as _**h**_ , and the direct input to the PLM sub-module that computes _**h**_ as _**x**_ (e.g. _**h**_ and _**x**_ can be the attention output and input respectively). To characterize this modification process, we define a set of design dimensions, and different methods can be instantiated by varying values along these dimensions. We detail the design dimensions below, and illustrate how adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA fall along them in Table 1:

**Functional Form** is the specific function that computes ∆ _**h**_ . We have detailed the functional form for adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA in Eq. 4, 6, and 10 respectively. The functional forms of all these methods are similar with a proj ~~d~~ own _→_ nonlinear _→_ proj ~~u~~ p architecture, while “nonlinear” degenerates to the identity function in LoRA.

**Modified Representation** indicates which hidden representation is directly modified.[8]

**Insertion Form** is how the added module is inserted into the network. As mentioned in the previous section and shown in Figure 3, traditionally adapters are inserted at a position in a sequential manner, where both the input and output are _**h**_ . Prefix tuning and LoRA – although not originally described in this way – turn out to be equivalent to a parallel insertion where _**x**_ is the input.

**Composition Function** is how the modified vector ∆ _**h**_ is composed with the original hidden representation _**h**_ to form the new hidden representation. For example, adapters perform simple additive composition, prefix tuning uses a gated additive composition as shown in Eq. 10, and LoRA scales ∆ _**h**_ by a constant factor and adds it to the original hidden representation as in Eq. 6.

We note that many other methods not present in Table 1 fit into this framework as well. For example, prompt tuning modifies the head attention in the first layer in a way similar to prefix tuning, and various adapter variants (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021) can be represented in a similar way as adapters. Critically, the unified framework allows us to study parameter-efficient tuning methods along these design dimensions, identify the critical design choices, and potentially transfer design elements across approaches, as in the following section.

## 3.3 TRANSFERRING DESIGN ELEMENTS

Here, and in Figure 3, we describe just a few novel methods that can be derived through our unified view above by transferring design elements across methods: (1) _Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the parallel insertion of prefix tuning into adapters. Interestingly, while we motivate the parallel adapter due to its similarity to prefix tuning, concurrent work (Zhu et al., 2021) independently proposed this variant and studied it empirically; (2) _Multi-head Parallel Adapter_ is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning. This way the variant improves the capacity for free by utilizing the multi-head projections as we discuss in §3.1. (3) _Scaled Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the composition and insertion form of LoRA into adapters, as shown in Figure 3e.

Our discussion and formulation so far raise a few questions: Do methods varying the design elements above exhibit distinct properties? Which design dimensions are particularly important? Do the novel methods described above yield better performance? We answer these questions next.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 GENERAL SETUP

**Datasets:** We study four downstream tasks: (1) XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) is an English summarization dataset where models predict a summary given a news article; (2) English to Romanian translation using the WMT 2016 en-ro dataset (Bojar et al., 2016); (3) MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) is an English natural language inference dataset where models predict whether one sentence entails, contradicts, or is neutral to another. (4) SST2 (Socher et al., 2013) is an English sentiment classification benchmark where models predict whether a sentence’s sentiment is positive or negative.

**Setup:** We use BARTLARGE (Lewis et al., 2020) and a multilingual version of it, mBARTLARGE (Liu et al., 2020a), as the underlying pretrained models for XSum and en-ro translation respectively, and we use RoBERTaBASE (Liu et al., 2019) for MNLI and SST2. We vary the bottleneck dimension within _{_ 1 _,_ 30 _,_ 200 _,_ 512 _,_ 1024 _}_ if needed.[9] We mainly study adapters, prefix tuning (prefix), and LoRA which greatly outperform bitfit and prompt tuning in our experiments. In the analysis sections (§4.3-4.5) we insert adapters _either_ at the attention or FFN layers for easier analysis, but include the results of inserting at both places in the final comparison (§4.6). We re-implement these methods based on their respective public code.[10] We use the huggingface transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020) for our implementation. Complete setup details can be found in Appendix A.

**Evaluation:** We report ROUGE 1/2/L scores (R-1/2/L, Lin (2004)) on the XSum test set, BLEU scores (Papineni et al., 2002) on the en-ro test set, and accuracy on the MNLI and SST2 dev set. For MNLI and SST2, we take the median of five random runs. We also report the number of tuned parameters relative to that in full fine-tuning (#params).

## 4.2 THE RESULTS OF EXISTING METHODS

We first overview the results of existing methods on the four tasks. As shown in Figure 4 and Table 2, while existing methods can achieve competitive performance on MNLI and SST2 by tuning fewer than 1% parameters, a large gap is still present if we add 5% parameters in XSum and en-ro. The gap remains significant even though we increase the relative parameter size to _>_ 10%. Even larger gaps have been observed in Raffel et al. (2020) on high-resource MT tasks. This shows that many methods that claimed comparable results to full fine-tuning on the GLUE benchmark with an encoder-only model (Guo et al., 2021; Ben Zaken et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021), or on relatively simple generation benchmarks such as E2E (Novikova et al., 2017) with an encoder-decoder model (Li & Liang, 2021), may not generalize well to other standard benchmarks. The influencing factors could be complicated including the number of training samples, task complexity, or model architecture. We thus advocate for future research on this line to report results on more diverse benchmarks to exhibit a more complete picture of their performance profile. Below, our analysis will mainly focus on the XSum and en-ro datasets to better distinguish different design choices. We note that these two benchmarks are relatively high-resource performed with an encoder-decoder model (BART), while we will discuss the results on MNLI and SST2 with an encoder-only model (RoBERTa) in §4.6.

## 4.4 WHICH MODIFIED REPRESENTATION – ATTENTION OR FFN?

**Setup:** We now study the effect of modifying different representations. We mainly compare attention and FFN modification. For easier analysis we categorize methods that modifies any hidden representations in the attention sub-layer (e.g. the head output, query, etc) as modifying the attention module. We compare parallel adapters at attention and FFN and prefix tuning. We also transfer the FFN modification to LoRA to have a LoRA (ffn) variant for a complete comparison. Specifically, we use LoRA to approximate the parameter updates for the FFN weights _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][×][d][m]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][m][×][d]_ . In this case _**W**_ up in LoRA for _**W**_ 1 (similar for _**W**_ down of _**W**_ 2) would have dimensions of _r × dm_ , where _dm_ = 4 _d_ as described in §2.1. Thus we typically use smaller _r_ for LoRA (ffn) than other methods to match their overall parameter size in later experiments.

**Results:** As shown in Figure 5, _any_ method with FFN modification outperforms _all_ the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params), often with fewer parameters. Second, the same method applied at FFN always improves over its attention counterpart. For example, LoRA (ffn) improves LoRA (attn) by 1 R-2 points on XSum. We also highlight that prefix tuning does not keep improving when we further increase the capacity, which is also observed in Li & Liang (2021). These results suggest that _FFN modification can utilize the added parameters more effectively than attention, no matter what the functional form or composition function is_ . We hypothesize that this is because the FFN learns task-specific textual patterns (Geva et al., 2021), while attention learns pairwise positional interactions which do not require large capacity for adapting to new tasks.

**Is the story different when we use** 0 _._ 1% **parameters?** In §3.1 we reason that prefix tuning is more expressive than adapters (attn), which, however, is not reflected in Figure 5. We conjecture that this is because multi-head attention is only superior when the parameter budget is small. To validate this hypothesis, we compare prefix tuning to parallel adapters when they add 0 _._ 1% of the pretrained parameters. To ablate the impact of the composition function, we also report the results of removing the gating in prefix tuning as _**h**_ + ∆ _**h**_ . We include the results of the multi-head parallel adapter variant (MH PA) described in §3.3. As shown in Table 4, the multi-head methods – prefix tuning and MH PA (attn) – outperform all others by at least 1.6 BLEU points when using 0.1% of the parameters. Surprisingly, reducing _l_ from 200 to 30 only causes 0.4 BLEU loss for prefix tuning while PA (attn) loses 1.9 points. The gating composition function in prefix tuning slightly helps the results by 0.3 points. We highlight that the MH parallel adapter improves the single-headed version by 1.6 points, which again verifies the effectiveness of the multi-head formulation.

Combining the results in Figure 5 and Table 4, we conclude that _modifying head attention shows the best results when the parameter budget is very small, while the FFN can better utilize modifications at larger capacities._ This suggests that it may be effective to allocate a larger parameter budget to FFN modification instead of treating attention and FFN equally as in Houlsby et al. (2019).

## 4.5 WHICH COMPOSITION FUNCTION?

## 4.6 AN EFFECTIVE INTEGRATION BY TRANSFERRING FAVORABLE DESIGN ELEMENTS

We first highlight three findings in previous sections: (1) Scaled parallel adapter is the best variant to modify FFN; (2) FFN can better utilize modification at larger capacities; and (3) modifying head attentions like prefix tuning can achieve strong performance with only 0.1% parameters. Inspired by them, we mix and match the favorable designs behind these findings: specifically, we use prefix tuning with a small bottleneck dimension ( _l_ = 30) at the attention sub-layers and allocate more parameter budgets to modify FFN representation using the scaled parallel adapter ( _r_ = 512). Since prefix tuning can be viewed as a form of adapter in our unified framework, we name this variant as _Mix-And-Match adapter (MAM Adapter)_ . In Table 6, we compare MAM adapter with various parameter-efficient tuning methods. For completeness, we also present results of other combination versions in Table 6: using parallel adapters at both attention and FFN layers and combining prefix tuning (attn) with LoRA (ffn) – both of these combined versions can improve over their respective prototypes. However, MAM Adapter achieves the best performance on both tasks and is able to match the results of our full fine-tuning by only updating 6 _._ 7% of the pre-trained parameters. In Table 2, we present the results of MAM Adapter on MNLI and SST2 as well, where MAM Adapter achieves comparable results to full fine-tuning by adding only 0.5% of pretrained parameters.

## 5 DISCUSSION

We provide a unified framework for several performant parameter-tuning methods, which enables us to instantiate a more effective model that matches the performance of full fine-tuning method through transferring techniques across approaches. We hope our work can provide insights and guidance for future research on parameter-efficient tuning.

## ETHICS STATEMENT

Our work proposes a method for efficient fine-tuning of pre-trained models, in particular language models. Pre-trained language models have a wide variety of positive applications, such as the applications to summarization, translation, or language understanding described in our paper. At the same time, there are a number of ethical concerns with language models in general, including concerns regarding the generation of biased or discriminative text (Bordia & Bowman, 2019), the leakage of private information from training data (Carlini et al., 2020), and environmental impact of training or tuning them (Strubell et al., 2019).

Our method attempts to train language models making minimal changes to their pre-existing parameters. While it is an interesting research question whether parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods exacerbate, mitigate, or make little change to issues such as bias or information leakage, to our knowledge no previous work has examined this topic. It is an interesting avenue for future work.

With respect to environmental impact, the methods proposed in this paper add a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge; the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning. On the other hand, as the methods proposed in this paper may obviate the need for full fine-tuning, this may also significantly reduce the cost (in terms of memory/deployed servers) of serving models. Notably, the great majority of the experimentation done for this paper was performed on a data center powered entirely by renewable energy.

## REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

In addition to the setup description in §4.1, we have detailed the complete experiments setup such as batch size, optimizer, learning rates in Appendix A. Besides, we have publicized our source code. These resources should be sufficient to reproduce results of the paper.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and timely problem: understanding the relationships among the proliferating set of parameter-efficient transfer learning methods (adapters, prefix tuning, LoRA, etc.) and identifying the design choices that drive their effectiveness. The contribution is well-motivated and the framing—deriving an alternative form of prefix tuning to reveal its structural similarity to adapters, then building a unified framework along shared design axes (modification function, insertion position, composition method)—is conceptually elegant and likely to be influential for the community. The abstract and introduction are clearly written, the research questions are crisply articulated, and Figure 2 effectively motivates the work by showing that existing methods still trail full fine-tuning on harder tasks. However, several concerns warrant attention. First, the novelty risks being perceived as primarily unificatory/analytical rather than methodological; the authors should sharpen claims about the "new variants" to make clear what is genuinely new versus a recombination of existing ingredients, and quantify the gains attributable to each identified design choice through careful ablations. Second, the evaluation breadth (MT, summarization, classification, GLUE) is a strength, but the claim of "matching full fine-tuning on all four tasks" needs to be supported with statistical significance, multiple seeds, and fair parameter-matched baselines—otherwise small gains may not be meaningful. Third, the framework's generality should be stress-tested: it is unclear whether the unified view accommodates more recent or structurally different methods (e.g., BitFit, IA³, compacter) beyond surface-level mapping, and the paper would benefit from discussing limitations or cases where the abstraction breaks down. Finally, clarity could be improved by previewing the unified framework's key equations earlier and ensuring the derivation linking prefix tuning to adapters is mathematically transparent, as this is the linchpin of the paper's contribution.

comments.title: Claim that FFN modification is universally better may overstate evidence
comments.quote: any _method with FFN modification outperforms_ _all_ _the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params)
comments.explanation: The prose says 'any method with FFN modification outperforms all the methods with attention modification in all cases' but then immediately acknowledges 'the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params'. This is a contradiction within a single sentence: 'in all cases' plus 'the only exception is...' cannot both be true. A careful reader can resolve this as meaning 'almost all cases' but the phrasing is logically inconsistent. This weakens the sweeping conclusion drawn afterward that 'FFN modification can utilize the added parameters more effectively than attention, no matter what the functional form or composition function is' — since at least one configuration shows the opposite.

comments.title: Parameter percentages for Scaled PA vs LoRA appear inconsistent with earlier claims
comments.explanation: The text says 'We set r as 512 for adapters and 102 for LoRA so that their tuned parameter sizes are the same.' Given the earlier parameter counts (adapter at FFN uses 2·r·d; LoRA uses 4·r·d when applied to two matrices, but LoRA (ffn) as a single projection would use 2·r·d_m with r=102 ≈ adapter 2·r·d with r=512 since d_m=4d). The 6.1% figure is plausible, but Table 6 separately lists LoRA (ffn), r=102 at 7.2% of parameters. The same configuration (LoRA ffn with r=102) is labeled 6.1% in Table 5 and 7.2% in Table 6. This inconsistency in the reported parameter percentage for apparently the same method configuration could confuse readers trying to reconcile the two tables. It's possible the Table 5 experiments use a different setup (e.g., only encoder, or only certain layers), but this is not explained.

comments.title: Claim that Scaled PA advantage depends on scaling may be overstated from single data point
comments.quote: LoRA ( _s_ = 4) performs better than parallel adapter. However, the advantage disappears if we remove the scaling by setting _s_ = 1.
comments.explanation: The authors compare LoRA s=4 (R-2=21.31) vs LoRA s=1 (R-2=20.83) vs PA (R-2=20.98) vs Scaled PA s=4 (R-2=21.54). The conclusion that 'the scaling composition function is better than the vanilla additive one' is supported by Scaled PA > PA. However, the specific claim that LoRA's advantage 'disappears' when s=1 is based on LoRA s=1 (20.83) being slightly worse than PA (20.98), which is a 0.15 R-2 difference — quite small and not clearly meaningful without variance information. The broader conclusion about scaling being beneficial does hold via Scaled PA vs PA (0.56 improvement), so this is a minor framing concern rather than a major issue.

With l = r these match, so the claim holds at face value. However, the preceding sentence emphasizes that prefix tuning operates per-head with full-rank updates to each head when l ≥ d/N_h, which implicitly uses the decomposition W_1 ∈ R^{d_h×l}, W_2 ∈ R^{l×d_h} per head — giving 2·l·d_h·N_h = 2·l·d total, consistent with the above. So the parameter counting is actually correct. The subtlety is that 'not adding more parameters' is true despite prefix tuning being 'more expressive' per-head, which is the authors' intended point. progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated, timely, and potentially high-impact: it addresses an important practical and conceptual problem in NLP—understanding parameter-efficient transfer learning methods beyond isolated empirical comparisons—and the proposed unifying perspective appears valuable for both analysis and method design. The introduction is clear, logically structured, and effectively establishes the gap between the proliferation of methods such as adapters, prefix/prompt tuning, LoRA, and BitFit and the limited understanding of their shared mechanisms and critical design choices. The paper’s main strengths are its ambition to provide a common framework, its focus on decomposing methods along interpretable dimensions, and its promise of broad empirical validation across summarization, MT, classification, and language understanding. That said, the most significant issue to watch is whether the claimed “unified view” is genuinely explanatory rather than primarily a post-hoc reparameterization: the paper will need to show that the framework yields nontrivial insights, fair comparisons, and predictive guidance for new method design, not just a taxonomy. The empirical claims also raise concerns about comparability, since parameter-efficient methods are often sensitive to architecture, optimization, parameter budgets, and task scale; rigorous controls and transparent hyperparameter tuning will be essential. Clarity is generally strong, though the opening could more sharply distinguish storage efficiency, training efficiency, inference cost, and number of trainable parameters, as these are related but not identical notions of efficiency. Overall, the paper appears strong and well written, with a compelling research question and promising contributions, but its ultimate quality depends on the depth of the theoretical connection and the fairness and diagnostic value of the empirical analysis.

comments.title: LoRA table appears to contradict the text about which attention projections are modified
comments.quote: |LoRA|**_xW_**down**_W_**up|parallel|attn key/val|**_h_**_←_**_h_**+_s ·_∆**_h_**|
comments.explanation: The surrounding prose states that LoRA is applied to “the query and value projection matrices (_**W** q,_ _**W** v_)”, which is also the common setting emphasized in Hu et al. (2021). If the table’s “attn key/val” entry is taken literally, it says that LoRA modifies key and value representations, not query and value representations. Applying LoRA to W_q versus W_k changes the attention scores in different ways and affects reproducibility. If the intended entry was “attn q/val” or “attn query/val,” the table should be corrected; if the experiments actually used key/value or query/key/value projections, the prose should say so.

comments.title: Calling the prefix factor matrices themselves “lowrank” is mathematically imprecise
comments.quote: Further, we note that _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][h][×][l]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[l][×][d][h]_ are lowrank matrices when _l_ is small, and thus they function similarly to the _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up matrices in adapters.
comments.explanation: The intended analogy is that the prefix-derived computation factors through an l-dimensional bottleneck, just as an adapter factors through bottleneck dimension r. However, W_1 and W_2, as rectangular matrices of shapes d_h × l and l × d_h, are not necessarily “low-rank matrices” individually; each can be full rank relative to its smaller dimension. What is rank-limited is the induced map/product or the span of possible modification vectors. This is a localized wording issue, but a careful reader could otherwise infer a stronger algebraic constraint on the individual matrices than is actually present.

comments.title: The contrast between prefix “weighted addition” and adapter “unweighted” addition is ambiguous
comments.quote: which reaches a very similar form to the adapter function in Eq. 4, except that prefix tuning is performing weighted addition while the adapter one is unweighted.[6]
comments.explanation: The high-level point—that prefix tuning and adapters can be viewed as similar plug-in modules—is helpful, and later context clarifies that prefix tuning has a softmax/gating structure whereas standard adapters use a simple residual addition. What remains ambiguous is what “unweighted” refers to. If it refers to the internal computation of the modification vector, the adapter expression f(_**hW**_down)_**W**_up is also a weighted linear combination of rows/basis vectors of _**W**_up, with weights given by f(_**hW**_down). If it refers instead to the composition with the original hidden state, then the intended contrast is probably “gated/scaled residual composition” versus “plain additive residual composition.” As written, the sentence may mislead readers about the actual algebraic similarity between the two functional forms.

comments.title: Equation references for prefix tuning and LoRA appear swapped
comments.quote: We have detailed the functional form for adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA in Eq. 4, 6, and 10 respectively.
comments.explanation: The surrounding context indicates that Eq. 4 is the adapter update, Eq. 6 is the LoRA projection-output modification, and Eq. 10 is the alternative prefix-tuning hidden-state form. This is also confirmed a few sentences later, where the text says prefix tuning uses the gated additive composition in Eq. 10 and LoRA uses Eq. 6. Thus “respectively” makes the mapping incorrect: as written, it assigns Eq. 6 to prefix tuning and Eq. 10 to LoRA. The underlying framework is clear, but this could confuse readers trying to follow the formal definitions.

comments.title: “Adapters are always single-headed” is too strong as a general statement
comments.quote: Eq. 10 applies to each attention head, while adapters are always single-headed, which makes prefix tuning more expressive: head attention is of dimension _d/Nh_ – basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if _l ≥ d/Nh_ , but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if _r ≥ d_ .
comments.explanation: The intended comparison seems to be between standard adapters, which operate on the full model dimension after the attention or FFN sublayer, and prefix tuning, whose rewritten form applies separately to each attention head. Under that conventional setup, the rank-capacity intuition is reasonable. However, the absolute wording “adapters are always single-headed” is not a property of adapters as a method class, and the next section proposes a multi-head parallel adapter. A more precise statement would be that the existing/standard adapter placements considered so far are single-headed.

comments.title: Multi-head parallel adapter parameterization is underspecified for the “for free” capacity claim
comments.quote: _Multi-head Parallel Adapter_ is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning. This way the variant improves the capacity for free by utilizing the multi-head projections as we discuss in §3.1.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is understandable: instead of applying one adapter to the full attention output, apply adapter-like modifications separately to each head output, analogous to prefix tuning. However, the phrase “for free” depends on a specific parameterization that is not stated here. If each head receives its own bottleneck of size _r_ with matrices of shape roughly _d_h × r_ and _r × d_h_, the total parameter count across _Nh_ heads is comparable to a single full-dimensional adapter with bottleneck _r_. But if _r_ is interpreted as a total bottleneck split across heads, or if each head uses full _d × r_ projections, the parameter count and capacity conclusions change. The method description should explicitly specify the per-head dimensions and whether parameters are shared across heads.

comments.title: Reported 1.7 ROUGE-2 gain does not match Table 3
comments.quote:
with PA (ffn) outperforming SA (ffn) by 1.7 R-2 points on

XSum and 0.8 BLEU points on en-ro respectively.
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is supported by the table: PA (ffn) is better than SA (ffn) on both XSum and en-ro. However, using the Table 3 values as printed, the XSum ROUGE-2 difference is 20.66 - 19.98 = 0.68, i.e. about 0.7 points, not 1.7. The BLEU difference, 36.4 - 35.6 = 0.8, is consistent. This may be a typographical or OCR error, but as written the numerical claim contradicts the table.

comments.title: FFN-versus-attention conclusions are overstated relative to the plotted evidence
comments.quote: As shown in Figure 5, _any_ method with FFN modification outperforms _all_ the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params), often with fewer parameters. These results suggest that _FFN modification can utilize the added parameters more effectively than attention, no matter what the functional form or composition function is_ .
comments.explanation: The empirical observation appears directionally supported: in the reported settings, FFN-side modifications often perform better than attention-side modifications. However, the prose first uses universal language—“any,” “all,” and “in all cases”—while immediately acknowledging an exception. The broader conclusion that FFN modification is better “no matter what the functional form or composition function is” is also stronger than the experiments can establish, since only a limited set of methods, tasks, and parameter ranges are tested. The later design-rule conclusion that head attention is best at very small budgets while FFN is better at larger capacities should likewise be scoped to the reported experimental settings.

comments.title: The stated 0.1% parameter budget appears inconsistent with the reported bottleneck sizes
comments.quote: we compare prefix tuning to parallel adapters when they add 0 _._ 1% of the pretrained parameters.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text discusses reducing the prefix bottleneck from l=200 to l=30, and later Table 6 reports prefix tuning with l=200 as 3.6% parameters. Under the paper’s own per-layer count, reducing l from 200 to 30 should scale this to roughly 0.54% for the same architecture, not 0.1%; this is also consistent with Table 6, where adding an attention component with l/r=30 appears to add about 0.6% over the FFN-only setting. If the intended number is really 0.1%, the paper needs to explain what subset of layers or parameters is being counted differently; otherwise the numerical basis for the comparison is misleading.

comments.title: Scaling composition claims and final MAM scaling hyperparameter are underqualified
comments.quote: Therefore, we conclude that _the scaling_ _composition function is better than the vanilla additive one while being easily applicable._
comments.explanation: Table 5 supports the narrower claim that, for the selected FFN modification setting on XSum, a fixed scaling factor improves over the unscaled parallel adapter. However, the wording “is better” reads like a general conclusion about composition functions, even though the ablation is limited to one task, one modified representation, and selected bottleneck sizes. This also affects reproducibility of the final MAM Adapter: the final method description says it uses a scaled parallel adapter, but does not explicitly specify whether it uses s=4, a dev-selected s, task-specific scaling, or another convention. Because the scaling factor is shown to matter, the final variant should state it directly.

comments.title: Efficiency and serving-cost claims are underqualified
comments.quote: the methods proposed in this paper add a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge; the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The intended point is reasonable: these methods add relatively few trainable parameters, and task-specific parameter storage can be much smaller than full fine-tuned model copies. However, describing the impact as “nominal” is undercut by the example that MAM Adapter can require up to 150% of full fine-tuning training time. The inference-time claim is plausible but not substantiated with measurements or conditions; prefix length, bottleneck size, sequence length, batching, hardware, and implementation details can all affect overhead. Similarly, the claim that these methods may reduce serving cost in terms of memory or deployed servers depends on a multi-task serving setup where a shared frozen backbone can actually be reused across tasks; task-specific storage savings do not automatically imply reduced inference compute or fewer servers.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated, timely, and potentially high-impact: it addresses an important practical and conceptual problem in NLP—understanding parameter-efficient transfer learning methods beyond isolated empirical comparisons—and the proposed unifying perspective appears valuable for both analysis and method design. The introduction is clear, logically structured, and effectively establishes the gap between the proliferation of methods such as adapters, prefix/prompt tuning, LoRA, and BitFit and the limited understanding of their shared mechanisms and critical design choices. The paper’s main strengths are its ambition to provide a common framework, its focus on decomposing methods along interpretable dimensions, and its promise of broad empirical validation across summarization, MT, classification, and language understanding. That said, the most significant issue to watch is whether the claimed “unified view” is genuinely explanatory rather than primarily a post-hoc reparameterization: the paper will need to show that the framework yields nontrivial insights, fair comparisons, and predictive guidance for new method design, not just a taxonomy. The empirical claims also raise concerns about comparability, since parameter-efficient methods are often sensitive to architecture, optimization, parameter budgets, and task scale; rigorous controls and transparent hyperparameter tuning will be essential. Clarity is generally strong, though the opening could more sharply distinguish storage efficiency, training efficiency, inference cost, and number of trainable parameters, as these are related but not identical notions of efficiency. Overall, the paper appears strong and well written, with a compelling research question and promising contributions, but its ultimate quality depends on the depth of the theoretical connection and the fairness and diagnostic value of the empirical analysis.

comments.title: LoRA table appears to contradict the text about which attention projections are modified
comments.quote: |LoRA|**_xW_**down**_W_**up|parallel|attn key/val|**_h_**_←_**_h_**+_s ·_∆**_h_**|
comments.explanation: The surrounding prose states that LoRA is applied to “the query and value projection matrices (_**W** q,_ _**W** v_)”, which is also the common setting emphasized in Hu et al. (2021). I therefore first read the table’s “attn key/val” entry as a possible shorthand or OCR corruption. However, if taken literally, it says that LoRA modifies key and value representations, not query and value representations. That is a real technical distinction: applying LoRA to W_q versus W_k changes the attention scores in different ways and affects reproducibility of the reported configuration. If the intended entry was “attn q/val” or “attn query/val,” the table should be corrected; if the experiments actually used key/value or query/key/value projections, the prose should say so.

comments.title: Calling the prefix factor matrices themselves “lowrank” is mathematically imprecise
comments.quote: Further, we note that _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][h][×][l]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[l][×][d][h]_ are lowrank matrices when _l_ is small, and thus they function similarly to the _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up matrices in adapters.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended analogy: the prefix-derived computation factors through an l-dimensional bottleneck, just as an adapter factors through bottleneck dimension r. The following sentences also correctly discuss a rank limitation and basis-vector interpretation. The precise issue is that W_1 and W_2, as rectangular matrices of shapes d_h × l and l × d_h, are not necessarily “low-rank matrices” individually; each can be full rank relative to its smaller dimension. What is rank-limited is the induced map/product or the span of possible modification vectors. This is a localized wording issue, but a careful reader could otherwise infer a stronger algebraic constraint on the individual matrices than is actually present.

comments.title: The contrast between prefix “weighted addition” and adapter “unweighted” addition is ambiguous
comments.quote: which reaches a very similar form to the adapter function in Eq. 4, except that prefix tuning is performing weighted addition while the adapter one is unweighted.[6]
comments.explanation: The high-level point—that prefix tuning and adapters can be viewed as similar plug-in modules—is helpful, and later context clarifies that prefix tuning has a softmax/gating structure whereas standard adapters use a simple residual addition. What remains ambiguous is what “unweighted” refers to. If it refers to the internal computation of the modification vector, the adapter expression f(_**hW**_down)_**W**_up is also a weighted linear combination of rows/basis vectors of _**W**_up, with weights given by f(_**hW**_down). If it refers instead to the composition with the original hidden state, then the intended contrast is probably “gated/scaled residual composition” versus “plain additive residual composition.” As written, the sentence may mislead readers about the actual algebraic similarity between the two functional forms.

comments.title: Equation references for prefix tuning and LoRA appear swapped
comments.quote: We have detailed the functional form for adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA in Eq. 4, 6, and 10 respectively.
comments.explanation: I initially checked whether this was just an OCR or numbering artifact, but the surrounding context resolves the intended numbering: Eq. 4 is the adapter update, Eq. 6 is the LoRA projection-output modification, and Eq. 10 is the alternative prefix-tuning hidden-state form. This is also confirmed a few sentences later, where the text says prefix tuning uses the gated additive composition in Eq. 10 and LoRA uses Eq. 6. Thus the word “respectively” makes the mapping incorrect: as written, it assigns Eq. 6 to prefix tuning and Eq. 10 to LoRA. The underlying framework is clear, but this could confuse readers trying to follow the formal definitions.

comments.title: “Adapters are always single-headed” is too strong as a general statement
comments.quote: Eq. 10 applies to each attention head, while adapters are always single-headed, which makes prefix tuning more expressive: head attention is of dimension _d/Nh_ – basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if _l ≥ d/Nh_ , but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if _r ≥ d_ .
comments.explanation: The intended comparison seems to be between standard adapters, which operate on the full model dimension after the attention or FFN sublayer, and prefix tuning, whose rewritten form applies separately to each attention head. Under that conventional setup, the rank-capacity intuition is reasonable: prefix values for each head span at most an _l_-dimensional subspace of a _d/Nh_-dimensional head output, while a standard adapter spans at most an _r_-dimensional subspace of the full _d_-dimensional representation. What remains problematic is the absolute wording “adapters are always single-headed.” This is not a property of adapters as a method class, and the next section in fact proposes a multi-head parallel adapter. A more precise statement would be that existing/standard adapter placements considered so far are single-headed. As written, the sentence slightly overstates the architectural limitation and makes the expressiveness comparison sound more fundamental than it is.

comments.title: Multi-head parallel adapter parameterization is underspecified for the “for free” capacity claim
comments.quote: _Multi-head Parallel Adapter_ is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning. This way the variant improves the capacity for free by utilizing the multi-head projections as we discuss in §3.1.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is understandable from the preceding discussion: instead of applying one adapter to the full attention output, apply adapter-like modifications separately to each head output, analogous to prefix tuning. However, the phrase “for free” depends on a specific parameterization that is not stated here. For example, if each head receives its own bottleneck of size _r_ with matrices of shape roughly _d_h × r_ and _r × d_h_, the total parameter count across _Nh_ heads is comparable to a single full-dimensional adapter with bottleneck _r_. But if _r_ is interpreted as a total bottleneck split across heads, or if each head uses full _d × r_ projections, the parameter count and capacity conclusions change. The context in §3.1 suggests the authors intend the first interpretation, but the method description itself does not explicitly specify the per-head dimensions or whether parameters are shared across heads. This is a localized reproducibility/justification gap for a proposed variant.

comments.title: Reported 1.7 ROUGE-2 gain does not match Table 3
comments.quote:
with PA (ffn) outperforming SA (ffn) by 1.7 R-2 points on

XSum and 0.8 BLEU points on en-ro respectively.
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is clear and supported by the table: PA (ffn) is better than SA (ffn) on both XSum and en-ro. However, using the Table 3 values as printed, the XSum ROUGE-2 difference is 20.66 - 19.98 = 0.68, i.e. about 0.7 points, not 1.7. I also checked whether the 1.7 could be comparing PA (ffn) to SA (attn), but that gives 20.66 - 19.30 = 1.36, still not 1.7. The BLEU difference, 36.4 - 35.6 = 0.8, is consistent. This may be an OCR or typographical error where “0.7” was read or written as “1.7”; if so, the concern is resolved. As written, though, the numerical claim contradicts the table.

comments.title: Universal wording about FFN modification is contradicted by the stated exception
comments.quote: As shown in Figure 5, _any_ method with FFN modification outperforms _all_ the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params), often with fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors are making a reasonable empirical observation: in Figure 5, FFN-side modifications appear mostly stronger than attention-side modifications, and they even acknowledge an exception. The concern is that the sentence first states the result in fully universal terms — “any,” “all,” and “in all cases” — and then immediately notes that there is an exception. A careful reader can infer the intended claim is “almost all plotted configurations,” but the literal statement is false under the authors’ own parenthetical. This matters because the subsequent design conclusion relies on the strength of this comparison.

comments.title: Conclusion about FFN modifications is broader than the experiments shown
comments.quote: These results suggest that _FFN modification can utilize the added parameters more effectively than attention, no matter what the functional form or composition function is_ .
comments.explanation: The evidence presented does support the narrower claim that, for the evaluated variants on XSum and en-ro, FFN modification often gives better returns than attention modification. I checked the surrounding setup: the comparison includes parallel adapters, a transferred LoRA-at-FFN variant, and prefix/attention-style methods, mainly on two encoder-decoder generation benchmarks. However, the phrase “no matter what the functional form or composition function is” reads more universal than the experiment can establish. Only a small set of functional forms and composition functions are tested, and the authors themselves later discuss MNLI/SST2 separately. A safer formulation would scope the conclusion to the tested methods, tasks, and parameter ranges.

comments.title: The stated 0.1% parameter budget appears inconsistent with the reported bottleneck sizes
comments.quote: we compare prefix tuning to parallel adapters when they add 0 _._ 1% of the pretrained parameters.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a small-budget comparison using the same parameter-count conventions defined earlier. The surrounding text then discusses reducing the prefix bottleneck from l=200 to l=30, and later Table 6 reports prefix tuning with l=200 as 3.6% parameters. Under the paper’s own per-layer count, reducing l from 200 to 30 should scale this to roughly 0.54% for the same architecture, not 0.1%; this is also consistent with Table 6, where adding an attention component with l/r=30 appears to add about 0.6% over the FFN-only setting. I considered whether this could be an OCR error for 0.5%, which would largely resolve the discrepancy, but as written the 0.1% figure recurs in the paragraph and supports the claim about extremely small parameter budgets. If the intended number is really 0.1%, the paper needs to explain what subset of layers or parameters is being counted differently; otherwise the numerical basis for the comparison is misleading.

comments.title: Bottleneck notation for PA is conflated with prefix length notation
comments.quote: Surprisingly, reducing _l_ from 200 to 30 only causes 0.4 BLEU loss for prefix tuning while PA (attn) loses 1.9 points.
comments.explanation: The intended comparison is clear: both prefix tuning and PA are being evaluated at smaller bottleneck capacity. However, earlier notation reserves l for the number of prefix vectors, while adapters and parallel adapters use bottleneck dimension r. Thus PA (attn) cannot literally have its l reduced. A careful reader can infer that the authors mean reducing the bottleneck dimension from 200 to 30 for each method, i.e. l for prefix tuning and r for PA. Still, the sentence blurs a distinction that is central to the paper’s unified framework, especially because one of the paper’s claims is that l and r are analogous but not identical implementation parameters.

comments.title: Small-budget conclusion is stated more generally than the presented evidence supports
comments.quote: Combining the results in Figure 5 and Table 4, we conclude that _modifying head attention shows the best results when the parameter budget is very small, while the FFN can better utilize modifications at larger capacities._
comments.explanation: The qualitative pattern the authors describe is plausible and fits their unified-framework motivation: head-wise attention modifications can be more parameter-efficient at very small ranks, while FFN modifications may benefit more from larger bottlenecks. My concern is with the breadth of the conclusion. In the immediate passage, the very-small-budget evidence is reported in terms of BLEU, apparently on the MT setting, and the larger-capacity conclusion depends on Figure 5, whose scope is not restated here. The wording reads as a general design rule across tasks and models, but the evidence shown in this section appears empirical and task-limited. A more qualified statement such as “in our XSum/MT experiments” or “under these settings” would better match what has been established.

comments.title: Scaling factor for the final MAM Adapter is not specified where it matters
comments.quote: we use prefix tuning with a small bottleneck dimension ( _l_ = 30) at the attention sub-layers and allocate more parameter budgets to modify FFN representation using the scaled parallel adapter ( _r_ = 512).
comments.explanation: The construction of MAM Adapter is mostly clear: prefix tuning is used for attention and a scaled parallel adapter is used for FFN. However, the preceding subsection emphasizes that the scaling constant s has a substantial effect: LoRA with s=4 outperforms s=1, and Scaled PA with s=4 outperforms both vanilla PA and trainable-s in Table 5. Given that, the final method description should specify whether MAM uses s=4, a dev-selected s, a task-specific s, or some other convention. A reader can guess that the authors reuse the best Scaled PA setting from Table 5, but because s is an important hyperparameter and part of the composition function being studied, leaving it implicit weakens reproducibility of the final variant.

comments.title: Composition-function conclusion is broader than the single ablation warrants
comments.quote: Therefore, we conclude that _the scaling_ _composition function is better than the vanilla additive one while being easily applicable._
comments.explanation: The ablation in Table 5 is useful and the reported numbers do support the narrower claim that, for FFN modification on XSum with the selected bottleneck sizes, a fixed scaling factor improves over the unscaled parallel adapter. What remains problematic is the unqualified phrasing “is better,” which sounds like a general conclusion about composition functions. The comparison is performed on one task, one modified representation, one adapter size, and with s selected on the dev set; it also does not establish whether the gain is robust across datasets or sensitive to the chosen scaling grid. A qualified conclusion would be more appropriate, e.g. that scaled addition was better in this FFN/XSum ablation and was therefore adopted in the final variant.

comments.title: Training-time overhead is described as nominal despite reaching 1.5× full fine-tuning
comments.quote: the methods proposed in this paper add a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge; the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: these methods add relatively few trainable parameters, so the per-step computational graph remains close to that of the underlying PLM, and the authors are trying to contrast this with the potentially large memory savings from not storing a separate fully fine-tuned model per task. However, the example given partly undercuts the characterization of the impact as “nominal.” A method requiring up to 150% of the training time of full fine-tuning is a substantial overhead in many experimental and environmental-impact contexts, especially since wall-clock training time is one of the main contributors to energy use. The sentence explains that the overhead comes from needing more epochs, which is helpful, but it still leaves the prose overstating how small the impact is. A more careful phrasing would distinguish per-step overhead from total training-time overhead, or report measured training/inference times explicitly.

comments.title: Inference-time claim is plausible but not clearly substantiated
comments.quote: the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The claim is plausible for small adapters and small-prefix configurations, and the authors’ broader architecture discussion supports why the added components should be lightweight relative to the base PLM. Still, the statement is made as an empirical-sounding conclusion without giving measurements or specifying the conditions under which “roughly the same” holds. Prefix-based methods can increase attention key/value lengths, and adapter-style modules add extra matrix multiplications; these costs may be small, but their practical impact depends on prefix length, bottleneck size, sequence length, batch size, hardware, and whether adapters are fused or implemented efficiently. Since this passage is specifically about environmental impact, a careful reader may expect quantitative inference-time evidence or at least a qualifier that this is based on the tested MAM configuration rather than all proposed variants.

comments.title: Serving-cost reduction claim conflates memory savings with deployed-server savings
comments.quote: as the methods proposed in this paper may obviate the need for full fine-tuning, this may also significantly reduce the cost (in terms of memory/deployed servers) of serving models.
comments.explanation: The intended argument is sound in the common multi-task serving scenario: if many tasks share one frozen backbone and store only small task-specific modules, parameter-efficient tuning can reduce model-storage and memory costs compared with keeping one full model copy per task. However, the sentence could be read more broadly than the evidence supports. The paper’s methods mainly reduce task-specific parameter storage; they do not necessarily reduce inference compute, and they reduce the number of deployed servers only under particular serving assumptions, such as many tasks being served concurrently and the shared backbone being reusable across them. If each task still requires the same backbone resident in memory on separate machines for throughput or isolation reasons, server-count savings may not follow. The “may” language helps, but the parenthetical “memory/deployed servers” remains somewhat underqualified.
